text: The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg with the operating table tilted fifteen degrees to the left. Robotic arms are mounted to the trocars, and robotic instruments are inserted for the restorative proctocoloctomy procedure.

The surgeon identifies the ileocolic pedicle by retracting the ileocolic junction anteriorly and laterally. The peritoneum overlying the pedicle is incised, isolating the structure. The ileocolic artery and vein are clipped and transected using the high ligation technique.

The surgeon performs a mesenteric dissection using a medial to lateral approach, staying between the embryological planes just anterior to Toldt's and Gerota's fascia and the duodenum. The hepatic flexure is dissected medially, and the dissection is extended up to the root of the middle colic vessels. The middle colic artery and vein are clipped and transected.

The surgeon uses robotic arms to apply traction on the transverse colon, entering the bursal mentalis and dividing the gastrocolic tissue from right to left up to the splenic flexure. The dissection is then redirected to the right lower quadrant, where medial traction is applied to separate the lateral attachments of the right colon.
The surgeon prepares the terminal ileum for transection, using an endoscopic linear stapler to perform the ileal transection.

The robotic system's axis is rotated from the right to the left side of the patient for the dissection of the left colon and rectum. The operating table is tilted fifteen degrees to the right, and the robotic arms are repositioned. Small bowel loops are retracted out of the pelvis and into the right upper quadrant, followed by the insertion of robotic instruments.

The surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon anteriorly and incises the peritoneum at the promontorium level to enter the aorta mesenteric window. The inferior mesenteric artery pedicle is identified and dissected while preserving the surrounding nerve plexus. The artery is clipped and transected, followed by the division of the inferior mesenteric vein using the same technique.

The surgeon dissects the mesentery of the left colon in a medial-to-lateral fashion, carefully preserving the left ureter, ornatal vessels, and autonomic nerves. Following the completion of the medial dissection, the left colon is retracted medially, and its lateral attachments are divided.
The surgeon takes down the splenic flexure, completing the colonic dissection. The robotic instruments are then directed towards the pelvis for further dissection.

The surgeon performs total mesorectal excision by dissecting the rectum, starting with an incision of the lateral peritoneal reflection below the sacral promontory. The rectum is mobilized posteriorly from the sacral hollow using sharp dissection between the fascia propria recti and presacral fascia. The posterior mesorectum is dissected first, allowing for easier mobilization of the right and then the left lateral sides of the mesorectum.
The surgeon performs dissection on the anterior plane, continuing the mesorectal dissection until reaching the anorectal ring, with visible separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon adjusts the angle of the robotic camera from a downward to an upward position to visualize the intersynthetic area, revealing the levator ani muscles and surrounding structures.

The surgeon transects the rectum using an articulating endoscopic linear stapler introduced through the suprapubic trocars. The specimen is extracted through an extended suprapubic incision. A J-pouch is constructed using a linear stapler, and an anvil of the circular stapler is placed at the distal end of the pouch. After re-establishing pneumoperitoneum, the circular stapler is introduced trans-anally to perform the pouch anal anastomosis.
The surgeon carefully ensures that the intestine is not twisted and the vagina is not interrupted in the staple line. A diverting loop ileostomy is created to complete the operation.
